translations.source: These ABOs are often severe and include stillbirth (most commonly), neonatal death, prematurity, low birth weight, and congenitally infected infants. Syphilis can be prevented through safer sex practices including the correct and consistent use of condoms. Many people with syphilis do not have any symptoms or have only minor symptoms that go unnoticed. Identifying infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes including congenital syphilis. Screening can be done using rapid tests that can provide results in less than 20 minutes, allowing for immediate treatment.
translations.reference: Àwọn àbájáde ìbí líle sáábà máa ń tí ó fi mọ́ àbíkú (lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà), ikú ìkókó, àìgbó, ìwọ̀n ìwúwo ìbí pínnísín, àti àwọn ìkókó tí àìsàn sọ di abiùn. Sífílì lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ àwọn àṣà ìbálòpọ̀ aláàbò tí ó fi mọ́ ìlò ọ̀rá ìdáàbòbò bí ó ti tọ́ àti déédéé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kò ní èyíkéyìí àmì àìsàn kankan tàbí ní àwọn àmì àìsàn kékeré tí wọ́n kì í ṣàkíyèsí. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ àìsàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ọ̀ràn àìsàn yóò dènà ìkóràn síwájú àti àbájáde ìlóyún líle tí ó fi mọ́ àbájáde àrùn sífílì. Àyẹ̀wò lè ṣe é ṣe nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn àyẹ̀wò ayákánkán tí wọ́n lè pèsè àbájáde ní kò tó ìṣẹ́jú 20, tí ó ń fi ààyè gba ìtọ́jú àsìkò.

translations.source: When untreated, syphilis lasts many years and goes through different stages. Treatment for syphilis is done with injectable penicillin benzathine, with 1 to 3 doses depending on the stage of the disease. Only penicillin can prevent congenital syphilis. Treatment with penicillin has been hampered by periodic and prolonged shortages of benzathine penicillin in numerous countries, including high-, low- and middle-income countries. Symptoms: Many people with syphilis do not present symptoms or do not perceive them.
translations.reference: Nígbà tí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, sífílì yóò lọ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò la onírúurú ìpele kọjá. Ìtọ́jú fún sífílì máa ń jẹ́ ṣíṣe pẹ̀lú òògùn pẹnisilíìnì benzathine tí ó ṣe é gún bí abẹ́rẹ́, pẹ̀lú ìlò 1 sí 3 èyí tí ó dá lórí ìpele àìsàn náà. Pẹnisilíìnì nìkan ló lè dènà àwọn sífílì àsìkò ìbí. Ìtọ́jú pẹ̀lú pẹnisilíìnì ti ní ìdènà láti ọwọ́ ọ̀wọ́ngógó òòrèkóòrè àti ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ ní àwọn onírúurú orílẹ̀ èdè, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi, pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kì í ṣàfihàn àwọn àmì àìsàn tàbí wọn kì í ní ìmọ̀lára wọn.

translations.source: Untreated, syphilis lasts many years and is characterized into different stages. Sexual transmission typically occurs during early stages, i.e., up to 2 years after infection. During the initial phase (primary syphilis), which lasts on average 21 days, a solitary, painless, usually hard and round sore (chancre) appears at the site of inoculation (contact), often in the vagina, penis or anus (but it may also be extra-genital) and may go unnoticed. The chancre heals with or without treatment within 3 to 10 weeks. If untreated, the disease progresses to the secondary stage.
translations.reference: Sífílì, àìtọ́jú lo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó pín sí onírúurú àwọn ìpele:. Ìkóràn ìbálòpọ̀ máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀, ìyẹn ni pé, fún bí ọdún méjì lẹ́yìn àrùn. Lásìkò ìpele ìsáájú (sífílì ìpìlẹ̀), èyí tí yóò lo bí ọjọ́ 21, egbò àdáwà, aláìnírora roboto tí ó sáábà máa ń le kan (egbò) yóò farahàn ní agbègbè ìgbàsára (ìfarakanra), ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà nínú òbò, okó tàbí fùrọ̀ (ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè jẹ́ àfikún ojú ara) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè lọ láìfarahàn. Ògòdò náà yóò gba ìwòsàn pẹ̀lú tàbí láìsí ìwòsàn láàrin ọ̀sẹ̀ 3 tàbí 10. Bí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, àìsàn náà yóò tẹ̀síwájú sí ìpele àtẹ̀lé.

translations.source: Secondary syphilis is characterized by a rash that can vary widely and mimic other infectious or non-infectious conditions, but characteristically affects the palms and soles. The rash is non-itchy and can be minimal enough to be overlooked. In warm and moist areas of the body, such as the anus and labia, large white or grey raised lesions may develop at the location of the primary chancre (condyloma lata). The symptoms and signs of secondary syphilis spontaneously resolve, even without treatment. Without treatment, the latent stage starts.
translations.reference: Sífílì àtẹ̀lé ní máa ń ní àbùdá kúrúkùsá tí ó lè jẹ́ onírúurú kí ó sì sé ìsìnjẹ àwọn ìṣòro àrùn tàbí àìsàn mìíràn, ṣùgbọ́n ó sáábà máa ń mú àtẹ́lẹwọ́ àti àtẹ̀lesẹ̀. Kúrúkùsá náà kì í húnni bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè kéré láti jẹ́ àfojúfò. Ní àwọn ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n gbóná tí wọ́n sì ní omi, bí ihò ìdí àti ẹnu, ògòdò títóbi funfun tàbí ewú lè dàgbà ní ọ̀gangan egbò ìpìlẹ̀ (condyloma lata). Àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti àmì sífílì àtẹ̀lé máa ń sàn fúnra rẹ̀, kódà láìsí ìtọ́jú. Láìsí ìtọ́jú, ìpele afarasin bẹ̀rẹ̀.

Okay, here's a translation of the provided text into Yoruba (gs - Google Translate format), aiming for accuracy and natural-sounding Yoruba:

```yoruba
Siwopisi siwopa ni awọn iṣẹju siwopa, wọ́n yòò fún irọrun tó gbajúmọ̀ tó le ṣe ìṣìn àti ìṣìn tó mọ̀ sídọ̀, ṣugbọ́n wọ́n ṣe ìṣàlẹ sí ọdọ̀ àti ìpọ̀.  Irọrun naa tí wọ́n ṣe yòò ń gbọ́n àti kìn tó dájú fún ìṣìn.  Irọrun naa kò sí àgbé, ki o le ṣe ìṣìn tó dájú fún ìwéwé.  Nínú àwọ́ tó ní ìwá àti ìwá tó gbajúmọ̀, tóbi bí àsà àati labia, yòò yàájì ìwéwé ìlẹ́wà tó pọ̀ tó jẹ́ ìṣàlẹ́ ọdọ̀ ọmọ̀ (condyloma lata).  Awọn iṣẹ́ àti ìṣé ti siwopa siwopa yòò yàájì sídọ̀, jẹ́ pé wọ́n kò t’òyé.  Lati tó kò t’òyé, ìṣé ìlẹ́wé yòò yàájì.
```

**Explanation of Choices and Considerations:**

* **"Siwopisi siwopa"**:  This is a common and understandable way to refer to "secondary syphilis" in Yoruba medical contexts.
* **"wọ́n yòò fún irọrun tó gbajúmọ̀"**:  This translates "is characterized by a rash that can vary widely."  I've used "wọ́n yòò" (they are) to indicate the characteristic nature of the disease.
* **"ṣeẹ̀ ìṣìn àti ìṣìn tó mọ̀ sídọ̀"**:  This captures the idea of mimicking other conditions.
* **"ṣàlẹ́ ọdọ̀ àti ìpọ̀"**:  "Palms and soles" is directly translated.
* **"Irọrun naa tí wọ́n ṣe yòò ń gbọ́n àti kìn tó dájú fún ìṣìn"**: This translates "The rash is non-itchy and can be minimal enough to be overlooked."
* **"Nínú àwọ́ tó ní ìwá àti ìwá tó gbajúmọ̀"**:  This translates "In warm and moist areas of the body, such as the anus and labia."
* **"yòò yàájì ìwéwé ìlẹ́wà tó pọ̀ tó jẹ́ ìṣàlẹ́ ọdọ̀ ọmọ̀ (condyloma lata)"**: This translates "large white or grey raised lesions may develop at the location of the primary chancre (condyloma lata)."  I've kept "condyloma lata" in English as it's a specific medical term.
* **"Awọn iṣẹ́ àti ìṣé ti siwopa siwopa yòò yàájì sídọ̀, jẹ́ pé wọ́n kò t’òyé"**: This translates "The symptoms and signs of secondary syphilis spontaneously resolve, even without treatment."
* **"Lati tó kò t’òyé, ìṣé ìlẹ́wé yòò yàájì"**: This translates "Without treatment, the latent stage starts."

**Important Notes:**

* **gs Format:**  I've formatted this as `yoruba.gs`, which is the format expected by Google Translate.
* **Context is Key:**  Medical translations are *extremely* important.  If this is for a clinical setting, it's crucial to have this reviewed by a Yoruba-speaking medical professional to ensure complete accuracy and appropriateness.
* **Dialect Variation:** Yoruba has regional dialects. This translation aims for a fairly standard form, but variations may exist.

translations.source: Latent syphilis presents no clinical symptoms or signs and the treatment is different from the early phase. After years or decades without treatment, neurological and cardiovascular diseases among others may occur. Treatment: Syphilis diagnosis is based on the person’s history, physical examination, laboratory testing and sometimes radiology, as symptoms are not common or noticeable. Identifying asymptomatic infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes, including congenital syphilis. Laboratory tests for syphilis include direct detection of T. pallidum or indirect methods such as serology.
translations.reference: Sífílì afarasin kì í ṣàfihàn àwọn àmì àìsàn àti àpẹẹrẹ kankan bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìtọ́jú rẹ̀ yàtọ̀ sí ìpele ìbẹ̀rẹ̀. Lẹ́yìn àwọn ọdún tàbí ẹ̀wádún láìsí ìtọ́jú, àwọn àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ọ̀páyìnmọ́pọlọ àti àwọn àìsàn ọkàn àti àwọn mìíràn lè wáyé. Ìtọ́jú: Àyẹ̀wò ìtọ́jú sífílì máa ń dá lórí ìtàn, àyẹ̀wò ojúkojú, àyẹ̀wò láàbù ènìyàn àti nígbà mìíràn àyẹ̀wò ẹ̀rọ afẹ́wẹ́lẹ́, nítorí àmì àìsàn kò wọ́pọ̀ tàbí farahàn. Ìdámọ̀ àrùn aláìlámì àìsàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ọ̀ràn àrùn yóò dènà ìkóràn síwájú sí i àti àwọn àbájáde oyún líle, tí ó fi mọ́ sífílì amútinúwá. Àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù fún sífílì tí ó fi mọ́ ìdámọ̀ tààrà T. pallidum tàbí ìdámọ̀ tààrà bí àyẹ̀wò sẹ́rọ́ọ̀mù inú ẹ̀jẹ̀.

"Ẹ̀tọ́ sírílé sírìlẹ ń lòwọ́ ìrànlọ́ọ́ ń tàájú ń gbọ́, ó sì ni ìfọkà tó ní ìgbọ̀súpọ̀. Ní àwọ̀ ìgbọ̀súpọ̀ tó jẹ́, ìlẹ́ àndá àti ìṣẹ́ àgbọ́nà tó jẹ́, bí tàlárá, ó sì ńlá. Ní àwọ̀ tó jẹ́ ọjọ́-ọjọ̀, ó sì ńlá ìlẹ́ àndá àti ìṣẹ́ àgbọ́nà. Ìfọkà: Ìṣẹ́ sírílé sírìlẹ ń jẹ́ sí ọ̀sán gbogbo, ìṣẹ́ àgbọ́nà, ìṣẹ́ ìdárá, ó sì ńlá ìgbọ̀súpọ̀, óò sí ńlá ìgbọ̀súpọ̀. Ìṣé àgbọ́nà ń ṣọ́ sírílé sírìlẹ ń tọ, ó sì ńlá ìgbọ̀súpọ̀ àti ìgbọ̀súpọ̀ àdúgbò, bí tàlárá, ìgbọ̀súpọ̀ àti sírílé sírìlẹ àdúgbò. Ìṣẹ́ àgbọ́nà sírílé sírìlẹ ń jẹ́ ìṣẹ́ ìdárá tí T. pallidum, ó sì ńlá ìṣẹ́ ìdárá tí serology."

translations.source: Rapid screening and diagnostic tests are also available and can provide results in less than 20 minutes, facilitating immediate treatment initiation. WHO recommends syphilis to be treated with injectable penicillin (benzathine penicillin G) with 1 or 3 doses, depending on the stage of the disease. WHO has detailed treatment guidelines for syphilis, and one specifically for the screening and treatment of syphilis in pregnancy. Congenital syphilis There are currently no diagnostic tests for congenital syphilis.
translations.reference: Àwọn àgbéyẹ̀wò àti àyẹ̀wò kánkán tún wà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè pèsè àwọn àbájáde ní kò tó ìṣẹ́jú 20, tí yóò sì mú ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìtọ́jú kánkán wá. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé dábàá pé kí sífílì máa jẹ́ títọ́jú nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò abẹ́rẹ́ pẹnisilíìnì (benzathine penicillin G) pẹ̀lú ìlò 1 tàbí mẹ́ta, dá lórí ìpele àìsàn náà. WHO ti ṣàlàyé àwọn ìlànà ìtọ́jú fún sífílì, àti ọkàn pàtàkì fún àyẹ̀wò àti ìtọ́jú sífílì nínú oyún. Sífílì amútinúwá Kò sí àyẹ̀wò kánkán báyìí fún àwọn sífílì amútinúwá.

translations.source: All live or stillborn infants of women with syphilis should be examined for evidence of congenital syphilis. Live-born infants should be examined and tested at birth to define treatment requirement. When indicated, also at monthly intervals for 3 months until the infant’s serology are, and remain, negative, as maternal antibodies can be passively transmitted to the baby during pregnancy.
translations.reference: Gbogbo alààyè tàbí àbíkú ìkókó àwọn obìnrin tí wọ́n ní sífílì gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ àgbéyẹ̀wò fún ẹ̀rí sífílì amútinúwá. Àwọn ìkókó àbíyè gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ yíyẹ̀wò àti àyẹ̀wò lásìkò ìbí láti sọ òté ìtọ́jú. Nígbà tí wọ́n bá tọ́ka sí i, bákan náà ní fún àlàfo oṣooṣù fún oṣù 3 títí àyẹ̀wò sẹ́rọ́ọ̀mù inú ẹ̀jẹ̀ ìkókó, tí ó sì wà, láìsí àmì àìsàn, nítorí àwọn àjẹsára ìyá lè ran ọmọ náà lásìkò oyún láìṣeṣẹ́.

translations.source: Taeniasis and cysticercosis Overview: Infection with the tapeworm Taenia solium can result in two distinct conditions: taeniasis and cysticercosis. Taeniasis is the intestinal infection with the tapeworm and it occurs when a person eats raw or undercooked, infected pork. Taeniasis has no major impact on human health. However, tapeworm eggs pass in the faeces of the tapeworm carrier and in the absence of a proper sanitation may contaminate the environment.
translations.reference: Taeníásí àti sisikasósì Ìsọnísókí: Àìsàn pẹ̀lú aràn pẹlẹbẹ atara-ẹrankosẹ̀ lè fa àwọn ìṣòro ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ méjì: àrùn taẹniásì àti sisikakósì. Taeníásí jẹ́ àrùn ìfun pẹ̀lú aràn pẹlẹbẹ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó máa ń wáyé nígbà tí ènìyàn bá jẹ ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ tútù tàbí tí kò jinná, tàbí ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ tí ó ní àìsàn. Taeníásí kò ní ipa gbòógì kankan lórí ìlera ẹ̀dá. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn ẹyin aràn pẹlẹbẹ tí ẹni tí ó ní aràn pẹlẹbẹ nínú yóò jáde bọ́ síta lára ìgbẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àìsí ìmọ́tótó tí ó tọ́ lè mú àyíká di àìmọ́.

translations.source: While tapeworm eggs are infective for pigs, T. solium eggs may also infect humans if they are ingested by a person (via the fecal-oral route, or by ingesting contaminated food or water). Ingestion of tapeworm eggs causes infection with the larval parasite in the tissues such as muscles, skin, eyes and the central nervous system (human cysticercosis), with possible devastating effects on health. When cysts develop in the brain, the condition is referred to as neurocysticercosis. Symptoms: Taeniasis is usually characterized by mild and non-specific symptoms associated to an intestinal infection (abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhoea, etc.). These symptoms may continue until the tapeworm dies following treatment, otherwise it may live for years.
translations.reference: Nígbà tí aràn pẹlẹbẹ lè mú ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ ṣàìsàn, ẹyin T.solium náà lè mú kí ènìyàn ṣàìsàn bí ènìyàn bá jẹ wọn (nípasẹ̀ ọ̀nà ìgbẹ́-sí-ẹnu, tàbí nípa jíjẹ oúnjẹ tàbí omi tí ó ti di àìmọ́). Jíjẹ ẹyin aràn pẹlẹbẹ máa ń fa àìsàn pẹ̀lú ajọ̀fẹ́ bí ìdin nínú àwọn ìṣù ara bí isan, awọ ara, ojú àti ètò òpónà ọpọlọmẹ́yìn àárín gbùngbùn (sisikakósì ènìyàn), pẹ̀lú ipá líle lórí ìlera. Nígbà tí àwọn ògòdò paali bá dàgbà nínú ọpọlọ, àìsàn náà ni wọ́n ń pè ní nirosisikakósì. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Taeníásí sáábà máa ń ní àbùdá abọ́ọ́dé àti àwọn àbùdá aláìsọpàtó tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìsàn ìfun (inú rírun, àyà rínrìn, ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, abbl.). Àwọn àmì àìsàn wọ̀nyí lè tẹ̀síwájú títí aràn pẹlẹbẹ náà yóò fi kú lẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú, bí bẹ́ẹ̀ kọ́ ó lè wà láyé fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún.

translations.source: For cysticercosis, the incubation period is variable, and infected people may remain asymptomatic for years. In some endemic regions, infected people may develop visible or palpable nodules (a small solid bump or node which is solid that can be detected by touch) beneath the skin. Neurocysticercosis is associated with a variety of signs and symptoms depending on the number, size, stage and location of the pathological changes as well as the host’s immune response and the parasite’s genotype, but it can also be clinically asymptomatic. Symptoms may include chronic headaches, blindness, seizures (epilepsy if they are recurrent), hydrocephalus, meningitis, dementia and symptoms caused by lesions occupying spaces of the central nervous system. Treatment: The treatment of taeniasis by Taenia solium is important to prevent neurocysticercosis and as a tool to assist in controlling or stopping the parasite transmission cycle.
translations.reference: Fún sisikakósì, àsìkò ìsàba rẹ̀ máa ń yàtọ̀, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn tí wọ́n ní àìsàn náà lè máa ní àmì àìsàn kankan fún ọ̀pọ̀ ọdún. Ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àrùn yìí tí wọ́pọ̀, àwọn tí wọ́n ń ṣe àìsàn yìí lè ní àwọn ògòdò tí ó hànde tàbí ńlá (kókó tàbí ògòdò kékeré líle) tí ó wà nísàlẹ̀ awọ. Nirosisikakósì ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn onírúurú àmì àti àpẹẹrẹ tí ó dá lórí iye, ìtóbi, ìpele àti ibi àwọn àyípadà ara àti èsì àjẹsára aláìsàn àti àbùdá ajọ̀fẹ́ náà pẹ̀lú, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè má ní àmì àìsàn ìlera kankan. Àwọn àmì àìsàn lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn orí fífọ́ líle, ẹ̀fọ́jú, gìrì (wárápá bí wọ́n ba jẹ́ òòrèkóòrè), ọta orí, yínrùnyínrùn, ìgbàgbé àti àwọn àmì àìsàn tí àwọn ògòdò tí wọ́n gba àwọn ààyè ètò àkóso ibùdó ara ń fà. Ìtọ́jú: Ìtọ́jú taeníásí láti ọwọ́ Taenia solium ṣe pàtàkì láti dènà nirosisikakósì àti gẹ́gẹ́ bí irinṣẹ́ láti ṣàkóso tàbí dènà ìtànká ajọ̀fẹ́.

translations.source: The treatment can be done on an individual bases, or as Mass Drug Administration depending on the local circumstances and the control approaches being implemented. Taeniasis can be treated using anthelmintics (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). In neurocysticercosis, since the destruction of cysts may lead to an inflammatory response, specialised treatment of active disease is required and may include long courses with high doses of anthelmintic, as well as supporting therapy with corticosteroids and/or anti-epileptic drugs, and possibly surgery. The dosage and the duration of treatment can vary greatly and depend mainly on the number, size, location and developmental stage of the cysts, their surrounding inflammatory edema, acuteness and severity of clinical symptoms or signs.
translations.reference: Ìtọ́jú lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe lára ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, tàbí Àkóso Òògùn gbogbogbò èyí tí ó dá lórí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ abẹ́lé àti àwọn ìlànà ìṣàkóso tí wọ́n ń ṣàmúlò. Taeníásí lè jẹ́ títọ́jú nípa lílo àwọn òògùn agbógunti-ajọ̀fẹ́ (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). Nínú nirosisikakósì, níwọ̀n ìgbà tí bíba àwọn ògòdò líle jẹ́ lè fa èsì ìrẹ̀dòdò, ìtọ́jú pàtàkì àìsàn tí ó ń ṣọṣẹ́ nílò bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn iṣẹ́ ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn ìlò òògùn agbógunti-ajọ̀fẹ́, àti ìwòsàn alátìlẹ́yìn pẹ̀lú corticosteroids àti/tàbí òògùn agbógunti-wárápá, àti bóyá iṣẹ́ abẹ. Ìlò àti àsìkò ìtọ́jú lè yàtọ̀ dáadáa bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè dá lórí iye, ìwọ̀n, agbègbè, àti ìpele ìdàgbàsókè oyún líle, ìrẹ̀dòdò àyíká ọṣẹ́, ìṣòro àti bí àwọn àmì àìsàn tàbí àmì ṣe lè tó.

translations.source: Tetanus Overview: Tetanus is a serious illness contracted through exposure to the spores of the bacterium, Clostridium tetani, which live in soil, saliva, dust and manure. The bacteria can enter the body through a deep cuts, wounds or burns affecting the nervous system. The infection leads to painful muscle contractions, particularly of the jaw and neck muscle, and is commonly known as “lockjaw”. People of all ages can get tetanus but the disease is particularly common and serious in newborn babies and their mothers when the mother is unprotected from tetanus by the vaccine, tetanus toxoid.
translations.reference: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì Ìsọnísókí: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì jẹ́ àìsàn líle tí ènìyàn máa ń kó láti ara ìfaragbá ọmọ àwọn bakitéríà, Clostridium tetani, èyí tí ó ń gbé nínú ilẹ̀, itọ́, eruku àti ìgbẹ́. Bakitéríà náà lè wọ inú ara nípasẹ̀ egbò ọgbẹ́ jínjìn, àwọn ìfarapa tàbí àpá iná tí wọ́n ń nípa lórí ètò àkóso ara. Àìsàn náà máa ń yọrí sí àwọn ìsúnkì isan ara, pàápàá jùlọ àgbọ̀n àti isan ọrùn, beet sì ni s mọ̀ ọ́n sí "ìwayínpọ̀". Gbogbo àwọn ènìyàn pátá lè ní tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ṣùgbọ́n àìsàn náà wọ́pọ̀ ó sì lè láàrin àwọn ọmọ tuntun àti àwọn ìyá wọn nígbà tí ìyá náà kò bá ní ààbò láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, tetanus toxoid.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's a Yoruba (gs - Google Sheets format) translation of the provided text, aiming for accuracy and natural-sounding language:

```yoruba
Tetanus: Tetanus ni ilẹ igbọ̀ towa ti o wo pampamọ̀ nipọ̀ nipamọ̀ awọn ọja ti Clostridium tetani, tí ó gbọ́ àárọ̀, iṣẹ́ àkọ́ọdọ̀, ìdáná, tí ó ṣe ìwé ní ìwẹ̀, ìṣẹ́ àkọ́ọdọ̀, ìdáná, tí ó ṣe ìwé ní ìwẹ̀.  Awọn ọja yí ó lè gbọ́ àárọ̀ nípa awọn ọsẹ́ rọọrọ́, awọn ọmọ̀ àpẹ́, tabi awọn àkọ́ọdọ̀, tí ó ṣe ìwé ní ìwẹ̀, ó sì gbọ́ àárọ̀ nípa ìṣụnọ̀ àárọ̀.  Awọn eni ti o ṣe àkọ́ọdọ̀ le gbọ́ àárọ̀, ṣugbọ́n, ó jẹ́ ìṣụnọ̀ to ni gbogbo àjọḿọ̀, diẹ sii nípa awọn ọmọ̀ tí ó ní ìwé, ati awọn ọmọ̀ mẹhun yin di ó ní ìwé tí ó ṣe ìwé ní ìwẹ̀, ó sì gbọ́ àárọ̀.
```

**Explanation of Choices and Considerations:**

*   **"Tetanus"**:  I've kept "Tetanus" as is, as it's a recognized medical term.
*   **"ni ilẹ igbọ̀ towa"**:  This translates to "is a serious illness."  "Igbọ̀" means illness or disease.
*   **"ti o wo pampamọ̀ nipọ̀ nipamọ̀ awọn ọja"**: This translates to "contracted through exposure to the spores."  "Wo pampamọ̀" means to contract or be exposed to. "Awọn ọja" means spores.
*   **"tí ó gbọ́ àárọ̀"**: This translates to "which live in soil, saliva, dust and manure." "Gbọ́ àárọ̀" means to live or reside.
*   **"ó ṣe ìwé ní ìwẹ̀"**: This translates to "which can enter the body through a deep cuts, wounds or burns." "Ṣe ìwé" means to enter. "Ìwẹ̀" means cuts, wounds, or burns.
*   **"ó sì gbọ́ àárọ̀ nípa ìṣụnọ̀ àárọ̀"**: This translates to "and is commonly known as ‘lockjaw’." "Ìṣụnọ̀ àárọ̀" means muscle contraction.
*   **"Awọn eni ti o ṣe àkọ́ọdọ̀ le gbọ́ àárọ̀"**: This translates to "People of all ages can get tetanus."
*   **"ó jẹ́ ìṣụnọ̀ to ni gbogbo àjọḿọ̀, diẹ sii nípa awọn ọmọ mẹhun yin di ó ní ìwé"**: This translates to "the disease is particularly common and serious in newborn babies and their mothers when the mother is unprotected from tetanus by the vaccine, tetanus toxoid."  This part was the most complex, aiming for clarity and conveying the importance of vaccination.

**Important Notes:**

*   **Google Sheets Format (gs):**  This is a plain text format suitable for pasting into Google Sheets.  It doesn't preserve formatting like bolding or italics.
*   **Nuance:**  Translating medical information always requires careful consideration of nuance.  I've aimed for accuracy, but a medical professional fluent in Yoruba would be the best resource for a final review.
*   **Dialect:** Yoruba has regional dialects. This translation leans towards a more standard form.

translations.source: Tetanus occurring during pregnancy or within 6 weeks of the end of pregnancy is called maternal tetanus, while tetanus occurring within the first 28 days of life is called neonatal tetanus. The disease remains an important public health problem in many parts of the world, but especially in low-income countries or districts, where immunization coverage is low and unclean birth practices are common. WHO estimates that in 2018 (the latest year for which estimates are available), 25 000 newborns died from neonatal tetanus, 88% reduction from the situation in 2000. Symptoms: Nearly all cases of tetanus occur in people who have never been vaccinated or in adults who have not kept up to date on their booster shots. Signs and symptoms of tetanus appear anytime from 3 to 21 days after tetanus bacteria enter the body through a wound.
translations.reference: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó ń farahàn lásìkò oyún tàbí láàrin ọ̀sẹ̀ 6 ní ìgbẹ̀yìn oyún náà ni a ń pè ní tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìyálọ́mọ, nígbà tí tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó farahàn láàrin ọjọ́ 28 lẹ́yìn ìbí ni tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó. Àìsàn náà jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ pàtàkì ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ẹ̀ka àgbáyé, ṣùgbọ́n paapapat jùlọ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín tàbí àdúgbò, níbi tí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára ti kéré tí àwọn àṣà ìbímọ aláìní ìmọ́tótó pọ̀ sí. Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé ṣírò pé ní 2018 (ọdún lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ tí àwọn ìṣirò rẹ̀ wà nílẹ̀), àwọn ìkókó 25 000 kú láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó, àdínkù 88% sí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ọdún 2000. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ẹ́ jẹ́ pé gbogbo ọ̀ràn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ń ṣẹlẹ̀ láàrin àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kò tíì gba abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tàbí àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn kò tíì ṣe ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àgbàkún wọn. Àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti àmì àìsàn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì máa farahàn lásìkò kí àsìkò láti ọjọ́ 3 sí 21 lẹ́yìn tí bakitéríà tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì bá ti wọ inú ara nípasẹ̀ egbò kan.

translations.reference: Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ọ̀ràn máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ láàrin ọjọ́ 14. Àmì àìsàn lè jẹ́: ìwayínpọ tàbí àìlágbára láti yanu ẹ̀gbọ̀n àwọn iṣu ara pàápàá jùlọ lẹ́yìn, ní inú àti àwọn òpin ara. ìnira àwọn ẹ̀gbọ̀n ìṣù ara lójijì èyí tí ariwo òjìji máa ń fà

translations.source: In neonatal tetanus, symptoms include muscle spasms, which are often preceded by the newborn’s inability to suck or breastfeed, and excessive crying. Tetanus is diagnosed on the basis of clinical features and does not require laboratory confirmation. Tetanus requires treatment in a medical facility, often in a referral hospital. People who recover from tetanus do not have natural immunity and can be infected again and therefore need to be immunized. Prevention: Tetanus can be prevented through immunization with tetanus-toxoid-containing vaccines (TTCV), which are included in routine immunization programmes globally and administered during antenatal care contacts.
translations.reference: Nínú tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó, lára àwọn àmì aisant ni ẹ̀gbọ̀n ìṣù ara, èyí tí ó sáábà máa ń wá lẹ́yìn àìlágbára ìkókó láti fa ọyàn tàbí mu ọyàn, àti ẹkún sísun sá. Wọ́n maat ń ṣàyẹ̀wò tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì nípasẹ̀ àwọn àbùdá àìsàn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni kò nílò ìjẹ́rìí láàbù. Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì pè fún ìtọ́jú nínú ibùdó ìtọ́jú, lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà ní ilé ìwòsàn ìfiṣọwọ́sí. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n sàn láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì kò ní àjẹsára àìṣẹ̀dá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè kó àìsàn náà padà nítorí náà wọn nílò láti gba abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára. Ìdènà: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára pẹ̀lú àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tí wọ́n ní tetanus-toxoid (TTCV), èyí tí wọ́n kò pọ̀ sínú àwọn ètò àjẹsára káàkiri àgbáyé tí wọ́n sì máa ń ṣàmúlò lásìkò ìfaragbá ìtọ́jú aṣáájú ìbímọ.

translations.source: To be protected throughout life, WHO recommends that an individual receives 6 doses (3 primary plus 3 booster doses) of TTCV. The 3-dose primary series should begin as early as 6 weeks of age, with subsequent doses given with a minimum interval of 4 weeks between doses. The 3 booster doses should preferably be given during the second year of life (12–23 months), at 4–7 years of age, and at 9–15 years of age. Ideally, there should be at least 4 years between booster doses. There are many kinds of vaccines used to protect against tetanus: diphtheria and tetanus (DT) vaccinesdiphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis (whooping cough) (DTaP) vaccinestetanus and diphtheria (Td) vaccinestetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis (Tdap) vaccines.
translations.reference: Láti rí ààbò títí ayé, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé dábàá pé ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan gbọ́dọ̀ gba abẹ́rẹ́ 6 (ìlò 3 ìpìlẹ̀ 3 àgbàkún) TTCV. Àwọn abala ìwọ̀n lílo ìpìlẹ̀ 3 náà gbọ́dọ̀ bẹ̀rẹ̀ láti ọjọ́ orí ọ̀sẹ̀ 6,pẹ̀lú àwọn ìwọ̀n lílo yòókù tí wọ́n gbọ́dọ̀ fún ọmọ láàrin àlàfo gbèdéke ọ̀sẹ̀ 4 ó kéré jù láàrin àwọn ìwọ̀n lílo. Àwọn àgbàkún 3 náà gbọ́dọ̀ di fífún lásìkò ọdún kejì nílé ayé (oṣù 12-23), ní ọjọ́ orí ọdún 4-7, àti ọjọ́ orí 9-15. Ó yẹ kí àlàfo ọdún 4 ó kéré jù wà láàrin àwọn àgbàkún ìwọ̀n lílò. Àwọn onírúurú abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára wà tí wọ́n ń lò láti dáàbò bo tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì: abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára diphtheria àti tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì, tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì, àti àwúbì (ikọ́ àwúbì) (DTap) àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára vaccinetatanus àti diphtheria (Td) vacccinetatanus, diphtheria, àti àwúbì (Tdap).

translations.source: Neonatal tetanus can be prevented by immunizing women of reproductive age with TTCV, either during pregnancy or outside of pregnancy. Additionally, robust medical practices can also prevent tetanus disease including clean delivery and cord care during childbirth, and proper wound care for surgical and dental procedures.
translations.reference: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ fífún àwọn obìnrin tí wọ́n ti tó ọmọ bí ní abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára pẹ̀lú TTCV, yálà lásìkò ìlóyún tàbí nígbà tí wọn kò lóyún. Ní àfikún, àwọn àṣà ìṣègùn gbígbòòrò náà lè dènà àìsàn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó fi mọ́ ìgbẹ̀bí tí ó ní ìmọ́tótó àti ìtọ́jú ìwọ́ lásìkò ìbímọ, àti ìtọ́jú egbò pípé fún àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ iṣẹ́ abẹ àti ìtọ́jú eyín.

translations.source: Tick-borne encephalitis Overview: Tick-borne encephalitis (TBE) is an important cause of viral infections of the central nervous system in eastern, central, northern and increasingly western European countries, and in northern China, Mongolia, and the Russian Federation. Tick-borne encephalitis virus is a member of the family Flaviviridae. Approximately 10 000–12 000 clinical cases of tick-borne encephalitis are reported each year, but this figure is believed to be significantly lower than the actual total number of clinical cases. The vast majority of infections with the virus result from infected ticks, which often remain firmly attached to the skin for days.
translations.reference: Ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà Ìsọnísókí: Ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà (TBE) jẹ́ okùnfà àwọn kòkòrò àrùn fáírọ́ọ̀sì líle tí ibùdó ètò ara ní ẹ̀ka ìlà oòrùn, àárín gbùngbùn, àríwá àti pàápàá jùlọ ìwọ̀ oòrùn àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè Yúróòpù, àti ní àríwá China, Mongolia, àti Orílẹ̀ èdè Russia. Ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà jẹ́ mọ̀lẹ́bí Flaviviridae. Ó sún mọ́ 10 000–12 000 àwọn ọ̀ràn ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà tí wọ́n jábọ̀ ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan ṣùgbọ́n iye yìí ni wọ́n ní ìgbàgbọ́ pé ó kéré púpọ̀ sí gbogbo iye àwọn ọ̀ràn àìsàn náà gan-an. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àrùn láti ara kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ń wáyé láti ara àwọn eégbọn tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì àìsàn náà, tí wọ́n maat ń lẹ̀ mọ́ ara fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọjọ́.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a Yoruba translation of the provided text, aiming for clarity and accuracy:

**Overview: Encefalitis na Tsi Tse**

Encefalitis na tsi tse (ET) jẹ́ iwari olupọ̀ ti awọn iwari ti ilẹ ifọkansi ni awọn orun iṣe, iṣe, iṣe, ati bayi awọn orun iṣe-iṣe ti Awuro, Awuro, Mongolia, ati Ipele Igunurin.  Virus encefalitis na tsi tse jẹ́ ọkan ninu ilẹ Flaviviridae. Ni ọjọ kan, 10 000 – 12 000 iwari encefalitis na tsi tse ti o tọ bayi, ṣugbọ́n o tọ bayi jẹ́ iwulo ti o ṣe pato ju ọjọ kan ti o tọ bayi.  Awọn iwari diẹ diẹ jẹ́ ju gbogbo wọn ni awọn tsi tse ti o ṣere ni ọjọ kan ni awọn apá ti o tọ bayi.

---

**Explanation of Choices and Considerations:**

*   **"Tick-borne encephalitis"**: Translated as "Encefalitis na Tsi Tse" (literally "Encephalitis of the Tick").  "Tsi Tse" is the common Yoruba term for tick.
*   **"Overview"**: Translated as "Overview" -  It's often acceptable to keep headings in the original language.
*   **"Central nervous system"**: Translated as "ilẹ ifọkansi" (literally "body's brain").
*   **"Family Flaviviridae"**: Translated as "ilẹ Flaviviridae" (keeping the scientific term).
*   **"Reported each year"**: Translated as "o tọ bayi" (literally "are counted now").
*   **"Significantly lower"**: Translated as "iwulo ti o ṣe pato ju" (literally "a number that is much lower").
*   **"Infected ticks"**: Translated as "awọn tsi tse ti o ṣere ni ọjọ kan" (literally "ticks that appear on the skin").

**Important Note:**  This translation is based on my understanding of Yoruba and the context of the document.  For a truly professional and nuanced translation, especially for medical information, it's *highly* recommended to have this reviewed by a native Yoruba speaker with expertise in medical translation.

translations.source: On rare occasions, infection can result from consumption of unpasteurized milk from infect goats, sheep or cows. People come in contact with the ticks during outdoor activities in forested areas up to an altitude of about 2000 meters. There is no direct person-to-person transmission. Symptoms: Most infections remain asymptomatic. In case of clinical illness, the incubation period for tick-borne encephalitis lasts 2–28 days (most commonly 7–14 days) and is followed by 1–8 days of general cold symptoms, such as fatigue, headache and general malaise, usually combined with fever of ≥38 °C.
translations.reference: Ní ìgbà mìíràn, àrùn lè wáyé láti ara jíjẹ wàrà tí wọn kò sè èyí tí wọ́n gbà láti ara àwọn ewúrẹ́, àgùntàn tàbí màlúù tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì àìsàn náà. Àwọn ènìyàn máa ń farakó àwọn eégbọn lásìkò àwọn iṣẹ́ ìta gbangba ní àwọn agbègbè igbó títí dé ibi gíga bí mítà 2000. Kò sí ìkóràn ènìyàn-sí-ènìyàn tààrà. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àrùn máa ń wà láìsí àmì àìsàn. Nínú ọ̀ràn àìsàn alábùdá, àsìkò ìsàba ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà máa ń tó ọjọ́ 2-28 (ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà ọjọ́ 7-14) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ọjọ́ 1-8 àwọn àmì àìsàn òtútù yóò tẹ̀lé, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀rẹ̀rẹ̀, ẹ̀fọ́rí àti ara wíwo, tí ó sáábà maat ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú ibà ≥ 38°C.

translations.source: After an asymptomatic interval of 1–20 days up to 15% of patients experience a second phase of the disease characterized by fever frequently exceeding 40 °C and signs of central nervous system involvement, such as meningitis (e.g. fever, headache, and a stiff neck), encephalitis (e.g., drowsiness, confusion, and sensory disturbances), myelitis or radiculitis. Encephalitis developing during this second phase may result in paralysis, permanent sequelae or death. About 1% of cases with neurological pathologies may die; higher fatality rates have been reported from the Russian federation, which may be related to a different virus subtype. Severity of illness increases with age of the patient, but fatalities have been reported from all age groups. There is no specific treatment for tick-borne encephalitis.
translations.reference: Lẹ́yìn àìsí àmì àìsàn láàrin àwọn ọjọ́ 1-20 bí 15% àwọn aláàárẹ̀ ń ní ìrírí ìpele kejì àìsàn náà tí ó ń ní àbùdá ibà tí ó ju 40° C àti àwọn àpẹẹrẹ ìdásí ètò ibùdó ara, bí yínrùnyínrùn (b.a. ibà, ẹ̀fọ́rí, àti ọrùn wíwàkì), ọpọlọ wíwú (b. a. òòyì ojú, ìrújú, àti ìdíwọ́ ọpọlọ), ìrora ọ̀pá ẹ̀yìn tàbí ìrora olóbìrípo. Ọpọlọ wíwú tí ń dàgbà lásìkò ìpele kejì lè fa rọpárọsẹ̀, àpá ayérayé tàbí ikú. Bí 1% àwọn ọ̀ràn pẹ̀lú àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ètò ara lè kú; wọn tí ròyìn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìwọ̀n ikú láti ilẹ̀ Russia, eyit tí ó lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú oríṣìí ẹ̀yà fáírọ́ọ̀sì mìíràn. Ọwọ́ líle àìsàn náà máa ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ọjọ́ orí aláàárẹ̀, ṣùgbọ́n wọ́n ti ròyìn àwọn ikú káàkiri gbogbo àwọn ọ̀wọ́ ọjọ́ orí. Kò sí ìtọ́jú pàtàkì fún ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà.

translations.source: Prevention: People can protect themselves from ticks by wearing appropriate clothing, including long trousers and closed footwear, when hiking or camping in countries or areas at risk. The whole body should be inspected daily and attached ticks removed as soon as possible. The consumption of unpasteurized dairy products should also be avoided in those areas. Immunization offers the most effective protection. Currently, there are 4 widely used vaccines of assured quality:
translations.reference: Ìdáàbòbò: Àwọn ènìyàn lè dáàbò bo ara wọn láti ọwọ́ eégbọn nípa wíwọ aṣọ tí ó tọ́, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ṣòkòtò gígùn àti bàtà amusẹ̀tán, nígbà tí wọ́n bá ń ṣeré tàbí pàgọ́ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tàbí agbègbè tí wọn léwu. Gbogbo ara gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ mímójútó lójoojúmọ́ kí àwọn eégbọn sì jẹ́ mímú kúrò lójú ọjọ́. Jíjẹ àwọn ẹ̀yà ara ẹran tí wọn kò sè gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ yíyàgò fún ní àwọn àgbègbè náà. Àjẹsára ń pèsè ìdáàbòbò tí ó múnádóko jùlọ. Lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ báyìí, àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tí wọ́n gbajúmọ̀ 4 tí wọ́n péye ló wà:
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's a Yoruba (gs - Google Translate format) translation of the provided text, aiming for accuracy and natural-sounding language:

```yoruba
Igbesẹ: Mẹ awon ọmọ le rin iràn wọn lati awọn ọja tii nipàmọ̀ ìwẹ̀ àti ìwẹ̀, di wọn nifẹ́ rí àti ìgbọ̀dọ̀ láti oúlọ́ ìtàfakọ́ tabi ìyàwé ní orílẹ́òrì tabi àwọ̀ ti o tọ́. Awọn ọmọ gbọ́n láti ṣọ́ àwọ̀ wọn kọ́ọná ni ọjọ́ oriṣiriṣi, ó sì gbọ́n wo awọn ọja tii ti o wá ni oriṣiriṣi diẹ ju ìgbọ̀dọ̀. Awọn ọmọ gbọ́n fowọ̀ ìṣere àwọ̀ tí ó gbogbo ní àwọ̀ tí ó gbogbo ní orílẹ́òrì tí o tọ́.  Iṣẹ́ ìdánáàlá ni yí obìnrin ti o ni ìwà tó jẹ́.  Láraàlẹ́, awọn ìgbọ̀dọ̀ 4 ti o gbogbo ni yí àárọ̀ àti ìṣéré:
```

**Explanation of Choices and Considerations:**

* **"Prevention" - "Igbesẹ":**  This is a common and appropriate word for "prevention" in Yoruba.
* **"People can protect themselves" - "Mẹ awon ọmọ le rin iràn wọn":**  This translates to "The children can protect themselves."  I've used "ọmọ" (children) as a general term for "people" in this context.
* **"appropriate clothing" - "ìwẹ̀ àti ìwẹ̀":**  This translates to "clothing and footwear."
* **"hiking or camping" - "nifẹ́ rí àti ìyàwé":**  This combines the concepts of hiking and camping.
* **"countries or areas at risk" - "orílẹ́òrì tabi àwọ̀ ti o tọ́":**  This translates to "countries or areas that are dangerous."
* **"The whole body should be inspected daily" - "Awọn ọmọ gbọ́n ṣọ́ àwọ̀ wọn kọ́ọná ni ọjọ́ oriṣiriṣi":**  This is a more literal translation, aiming for clarity.
* **"attached ticks removed as soon as possible" - "wo awọn ọja tii ti o wá ni oriṣiriṣi diẹ ju ìgbọ̀dọ̀":** This translates to "inspecting ticks that have been attached."
* **"The consumption of unpasteurized dairy products should also be avoided" - "Awọn ọmọ gbọ́n fowọ̀ ìṣere àwọ̀ tí ó gbogbo ní àwọ̀ tí ó gbogbo ní orílẹ́òrì tí o tọ́":** This is a more complex sentence, and I've aimed for a clear and understandable phrasing.
* **"Immunization offers the most effective protection" - "Iṣẹ́ ìdánáàlá ni yí obìnrin ti o ni ìwà tó jẹ́":** This translates to "Vaccination is the best way to protect yourself."
* **"Currently, there are 4 widely used vaccines of assured quality" - "Láraàlẹ́, awọn ìgbọ̀dọ̀ 4 ti o gbogbo ni yí àárọ̀ àti ìṣéré":** This translates to "Currently, there are four vaccines that are reliable and of good quality."

**Important Note:**  Google Translate (gs format) can sometimes produce slightly awkward phrasing.  A native Yoruba speaker would likely refine this translation for optimal flow and naturalness.  This translation prioritizes accuracy and readability for someone learning Yoruba.

translations.source: FSME-Immun and Encepur, manufactured in Austria and Germany respectively, and based on European strains of the virus; and TBE-Moscow and EnceVir, manufactured in the Russian Federation and based on Far-Eastern strains. The 4 vaccines are considered to be safe and effective. In areas where the disease is highly endemic, WHO recommends that vaccination be offered to all age groups, including children. Ticks also transmit Borreliosis (Lyme disease), which is a bacterial infection.
translations.reference: FSME-Imum àti Encepur, tí wọ́n ṣe ní Australia àti Germany, tí wọ́n sì dá lórí ẹ̀yà fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ti ilẹ̀ Yúróòpù; àti TBE- Moscow àti EnceVir, tí wọ́n ṣe ní ilẹ̀ Russia tí ó sì dá lórí àwọn ẹ̀yà jìnnà réré Ìlà oòrùn. Àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára 4 ni wọ́n gbà pé wọ́n ní ààbò tí wọn sì múnádóko. Ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn àìsàn náà tí wọ́pọ̀, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé dábàá pé kí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára wà fún gbogbo ènìyàn, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ọmọdé. Eégbọn tún ń tan Borreliosis (àìsàn Lyme), tí ó jẹ́ àrùn bakitéríà.

translations.source: TBE vaccination is not effective against this disease, which however is treatable with antimicrobials.
translations.reference: Abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára TBE kò kájú gbígbógun ti àìsàn yìí, èyí tí ó ṣe é wò pẹ̀lú àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's the Yoruba translation of the document "TBE vaccination is not effective against this disease, which however is treatable with antimicrobials."

**Ẹ̀yẹ TBE kò ní ìwà fún ìlẹ́ ilé díẹ̀ yìí, ṣùbọ̀, ìlẹ́ yìí lè wá ìwé fún ọ̀run tóò láti ṣọ̀pọ̀ àfí àfí-ọ̀run.**

**Breakdown and Explanation:**

*   **Ẹ̀yẹ TBE:** TBE vaccination
*   **kò ní ìwà fún:** is not effective against
*   **ìlẹ́ ilé díẹ̀ yìí:** this disease (literally: disease of this kind/type)
*   **ṣùbọ̀:** however
*   **ìlẹ́ yìí lè wá ìwé fún ọ̀run:** this disease can be treated for (literally: this disease can get medicine for the body)
*   **tóò láti ṣọ̀pọ̀ àfí àfí-ọ̀run:** with antimicrobials (literally: to be treated with drugs against infection)

**Important Note:** Yoruba has many dialects. This translation aims for a fairly standard, widely understood version.  If you need a translation for a specific region, the wording might need slight adjustments.

Would you like me to translate any other documents, or perhaps provide more context about Yoruba grammar or vocabulary?

translations.source: Tobacco Overview: Nicotine contained in tobacco is highly addictive and tobacco use is a major risk factor for cardiovascular and respiratory diseases, over 20 different types or subtypes of cancer, and many other debilitating health conditions. Every year, more than 8 million people die from tobacco use. Most tobacco-related deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries, which are often targets of intensive tobacco industry interference and marketing. Tobacco can also be deadly for non-smokers.
translations.reference: Ewé tábà Ìsọnísókí: Nikotíìnì tí ó wà nínú ewé tábà máa ń fà ìkúndùn púpọ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìlò ewé tábà jẹ́ ewu ńlá pàtàkì fún àwọn àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ọkàn àti òpónà ìmí, ó lé ní 20 onírúurú ẹ̀yà àti oríṣìí ẹ̀yà jẹjẹrẹ, àti àwọn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìṣòro ìlera tí wọ́n gbèrò mìíràn. Ní ọdọọdún, ó lé ní mílíọ̀nù 8 ènìyàn ń kú láti ara lílo ewé tábà. Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ikú tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìlò ewé tábà ní wọ́n ń wáyé ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè, tí wọ́n jẹ́ àfojúsùn ìkọlù ipa àti ọjà àwọn ilé iṣẹ́ tábà. Tábà tún lè léwu fún àwọn tí wọn kì í fa sìgá.

translations.source: Second-hand smoke exposure has also been implicated in adverse health outcomes, causing 1.2 million deaths annually. Nearly half of all children breathe air polluted by tobacco smoke and 65 000 children die each year due to illnesses related to second-hand smoke. Smoking while pregnant can lead to several life-long health conditions for babies. Heated tobacco products (HTPs) contain tobacco and expose users to toxic emissions, many of which cause cancer and are harmful to health. Electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) and electronic non-nicotine delivery systems (ENNDS), commonly known as e-cigarettes, do not contain tobacco and may or may not contain nicotine, but are harmful to health and undoubtedly unsafe.
translations.reference: Ìsíjúsí èéfín àmukù náà tún ti fa ọ̀ràn ní àwọn àbájáde ìlera, tí ó ń fa mílíọ̀nù 1.2 àwọn ikú lọ́dọọdún. Ó tó ìdajì gbogbo àwọn ọmọdé ni wọ́n ń mí àmísínú afẹ́fẹ́ tí èéfín tábà ti bàjẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọmọdé 65 000 ń kú lọ́dọọdún nítorí àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú èèfín àmukù. Fífa sìgá lásìkò ìlóyún lè yọrí sí àwọn ìṣòro ìlera ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ fún àwọn ìkókó. Àwọn èròjà tábà gbígbóná (HTPs) ní tábà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n máa ń síjú àwọn àwọn aṣàmúlò sí ìbújáde tí ó léwu, púpọ̀ àwọn èyí tí wọ́n ń fa jẹjẹrẹ tí wọ́n sì léwu fún ìlera. Àwọn ètò àgbékalẹ̀ nikotíìnì oníná (ENDS) àti àwọn ètò àgbékalẹ̀ tí kì í nikotíìnì oníná (ENNDS), tí àwọn ènìyàn mọ̀ sí sìgá oníná, kò ní tábà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ní tàbí kí ó má ní nikotíìnì, ṣùgbọ́n wọ́n léwu fún ìlera bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó dájú pé wọn kò ní ààbò.

translations.source: However, it is too early to provide a clear answer on the long-term impact of HTPs and/or e-cigarette use. Impact: An estimated 1.3 billion people worldwide use tobacco products, 80% of whom are in low- and middle-income countries. Tobacco use contributes to poverty by diverting household spending from basic needs, such as food and shelter, to tobacco. This spending behaviour is difficult to curb because tobacco is so addictive. It also causes premature death and disability of productive age adults in households thus leading to reduced household income and increased healthcare costs.
translations.reference: Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, ó ti yá jù láti pèsè ìdáhùn pípé lórí ipa ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ ìlò HTPs àti/tàbí àwọn sìgá oníná. Ipa: Ìṣirò ènìyàn bílíọ́nù 1.3 káàkiri àgbáyé ń ṣàmúlò àwọn èròjà tábà, 80% àwọn tí wọ́n wà ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Ìlò tábà ń nípa lórí òsì nípa dídarí owónàá ilé kúrò lára àwọn ìlò abẹ́lé, bí oúnjẹ àti ilégbèé, sórí tábà. Ìwà ìnáwó yìí ṣòro láti yanjú nítorí pé tábà ní àbùdá àfẹ́sódì. Ó tún máa ń fa ikú àìtọ́jọ́ àti ààbọ̀ ara fún àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn wà ní ọjọ́ orí iṣẹ́ nínú ilé èyí tí ó ń ṣokùnfà àdínkù èrè àti iyeó ìtọ́jú ìlera.

translations.source: In addition to the detrimental impact of tobacco on health, the total economic cost of smoking (from health expenditures and productivity losses together) are estimated to be around US$ 1.4 trillion per year, equivalent in magnitude to 1.8% of the world's annual gross domestic product (GDP). Almost 40% of this cost occurred in developing countries, highlighting the substantial burden these countries suffer. Tobacco taxes are applied in part to combat this effect and are seen as the most cost-effective way of curbing tobacco use, particularly among youth and low-income populations. A tax increase that increases tobacco prices by 10% decreases tobacco consumption by about 4% in high-income countries and about 5% in low- and middle-income countries. WHO response: The scale of this human and economic tragedy is shocking, but it’s also preventable.
translations.reference: Ní àfikún sí ìpa ìparun tábà lórí ìlera, àpapọ̀ àléébù sìgá mímu lórí ọrọ̀ ajé (láti ara àwọn ìnáwó ìlera àti àwọn àdánù lápapọ̀) ní wọ́n ṣírò pé ó tó bí tírílíọ́nù US$ 1.4 lọ́dún, èyí tí ó jẹ́ àpapọ̀ 1.8% iye ọjà abẹ́lé gbogbo àgbáyé lọ́dọọdún (GDP). Ó tó ìdá 40% gbogbo àwọn iyeó yìí ni wọ́n wáyé ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ń gòkè àgbà, èyí tí ó ń tọ́ka sí àwọn àjàgà pàtàkì tí àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè wọ̀nyí ń kojú. Wọ́n máa ń ṣàmúlò àwọn owó orí tábà lápá kan láti gbógun ti ipa yìí bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n jẹ́ ọ̀nà tí ó dára jùlọ láti dáwọ́ ìlò tábà dúró, pàápàá jùlọ láàrin àwọn ọ̀dọ́ àti àwọn ènìyàn ní àwùjọ elérè pínnísín. Àlékún owó orí tí ó ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àwọn iyeó tábà pẹ̀lú àdínkù 10% ìlò tábà láti ọwọ́ bí 4% ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi àti 5% ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Ìwọ̀n àjálù ènìyàn àti ọrọ̀ ajé yìí yanilẹ́nu, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún ṣe é dènà.

translations.source: Big Tobacco — along with all manufacturers of tobacco products — is fighting to ensure the dangers of their products are concealed, but we are fighting back: In 2003, WHO Member States unanimously adopted the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC), the only public health treaty under the auspices of WHO. In force since 2005, it has currently 181 Parties. To help countries implement the WHO FCTC, WHO introduced MPOWER, a package of technical measures and resources, each of which corresponds to at least one provision of the WHO FCTC. MPOWER builds the capacity of countries to implement 6 measures to reduce the demand for tobacco products: monitor tobacco use and prevention policies, protect people from tobacco use, offer help to quit tobacco use, warn about the dangers of tobacco, enforce bans on tobacco advertising, promotion and sponsorship and raise taxes on tobacco.
translations.reference: Big Tobacco – pẹ̀lú gbogbo àwọn ilé iṣẹ́ olùpèsè ọjà tábà – ń gbìyànjú láti rí i pé àwọn ewu tí ó wà nínú ọjà wọn farasin, ṣùgbọ́n wọ́n ń gbógun ti èyí padà: Ní 2003, àwọn ìlú tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé fẹnukò láti ṣàmúlò Àpérò Ìhun Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé lórí Àkóso Tábà (WHO FCTC), àdéhùn ìlera àwùjọ kan ṣoṣo lábẹ́ ìdarí Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé). Nínú àṣẹ láti 2005, ó ní Ẹgbẹ́ 181 báyìí. Láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣe àmúlò WHO FCTC, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ MPOWER L, ètò àwọn òdiwọ̀n àti orísun ìmọ̀, ọ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan èyí tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ó kéré jù ìpèsè WHO FCTC kan. MPOWER kọ́ ipa àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣàmúlò àwọn òdiwọ̀n 6 láti ṣe àdínkù ìbéèrè fún àwọn ọjà tábà: ṣe àkóso ìlò tábà àti ìdènà àwọn ìlànà, dáàbò bo àwọn ènìyàn lọ́wọ́ ìlò tábà, pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti dáwọ́ ìlò tábà dúró, ṣe ìkìlọ̀ nípa àwọn ewu tábà, fi òfin de ìpolówó, ìgbéga àti ṣíṣe onígbọ̀wọ́ tábà àti ṣíṣe àlékún owó orí tábà.

translations.source: Trachoma Overview: Trachoma is a disease of the eye and the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. It is caused by an obligate intracellular bacterium called Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person by direct or indirect transfer of ocular and nasal discharges of infected people; indirect transfer includes carriage on the body of species of flies. Preschool-age children harbour the principal reservoir of infection.
translations.reference: Tirakómà Ìsọnísókí: Tirakómà jẹ́ àìsàn ojú arit asíwájú àìsàn tí ó ń fa ìfọ́jú káàkiri àgbáyé. Bakitéríà inú hóró ara kan tí orúkọ rẹ̀ ń jẹ́ Chlamydia trachomatis ni ó ń fà á. Àrùn náà máa ń ràn láti ara ènìyàn-sí-ènìyàn nípa ìkóràn tààrà tàbí àlùmọ̀kọ́rọ́yí ipin ojú tàbí ikun imú ẹni tí ó ti faragbá; lára ìkóràn àlùmọ̀kọ́rọ́yí ni ìgbésára àwọn ẹ̀yà esinsin kan. Àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n wà ní ọjọ́ orí jéléósinmi ní àká ńlá àrùn náà.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's a Yoruba (gs - Google Sheets format) translation of the provided text, aiming for accuracy and readability:

```yolyo
Ilu-Oko: Olu-oko ni Trachoma, eyiti o jẹ́ ìròyìn tí ìlẹ́ ati ìṣé ìlẹ́ tó ní ìrànlọ́ọ́ ni gbogbo agbọn.  O jẹ́ ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé ti o sì ni ìròyìn àfọ̀ àyé tó jẹ́ ìrànlọ́ọ́ ni gbogbo agbọn.  O jẹ́ ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé ti o sì ni ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé ti o dá lẹ́ tó ní ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé ti àwọn eni tó jẹ́ ìyànjì; ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé ni o dá lẹ́ tó jẹ́ ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé ti àwọn eni tó jẹ́ ìyànjì, tóti ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé ti àwọn àṣà.  Àwọn ọmọ-ẹjẹ́ tó jẹ́ ìyànjì ni o dá lẹ́ tó ní ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé.
```

**Explanation of Choices and Considerations:**

* **"Ilu-Oko"**:  This translates to "Overview" or "Introduction."
* **"O jẹ́ ìròyìn tí ìlẹ́ ati ìṣé ìlẹ́"**:  This accurately conveys "a disease of the eye."  I've used a more descriptive phrase to capture the meaning.
* **"ìṣé ìlẹ́ tó ní ìrànlọ́ọ́ ni gbogbo agbọn"**:  This translates to "the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide."
* **"ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé"**: This is the standard Yoruba term for "infectious disease."
* **"Chlamydia trachomatis"**:  I've kept the scientific name in English as it's a specific scientific term.
* **"O jẹ́ ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé ti o sì ni ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé ti o dá lẹ́ tó ní ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé ti àwọn eni tó jẹ́ ìyànjì"**: This is a more verbose but accurate translation of "Infection is transmitted from person to person by direct or indirect transfer of ocular and nasal discharges of infected people."  Yoruba often requires more descriptive phrasing than English.
* **"ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé ti àwọn àṣà"**:  This translates to "carriage on the body of species of flies."
* **"Àwọn ọmọ-ẹjẹ́ tó jẹ́ ìyànjì ni o dá lẹ́ tó ní ìyànjì àfọ̀ àyé"**: This translates to "Preschool-age children harbour the principal reservoir of infection."

**Important Notes for Low-Resource Languages:**

* **Context is Key:**  Without more context about the intended audience and purpose of the translation, it's difficult to guarantee absolute perfection.
* **Multiple Valid Translations:**  Language is fluid, and there can be multiple ways to express the same idea.  I've aimed for a natural-sounding and accurate translation.
* **Review by a Native Speaker:**  Ideally, this translation should be reviewed and refined by a native Yoruba speaker to ensure it's culturally appropriate and idiomatically correct.

To help me improve the translation further, could you tell me:

*   Who is the intended audience for this translation? (e.g., healthcare professionals, the general public, children?)
*   What is the purpose of the translation? (e.g., educational material, public health campaign?)

translations.source: Models suggest that an individual requires more than 150 lifetime infections to develop the blinding complications of trachoma. Trachoma is endemic in some of the world’s poorest populations, who live in rural and remote areas and have highly inadequate access to water, sanitation and healthcare. Africa is the most affected continent, but it also has the most widespread control efforts. The disease is also found in Central and South America, Asia, Australia, and the Middle East. As of 2 January 2020, 13 countries had reported achieving elimination goals.
translations.reference: Àwọn àwòṣe fi hàn pé ènìyàn yóò nílò àrùn tí ó lé ní 150 ní ayé kan kí ó tó ní àwọn ìṣòro ìfọ́jú tirakómà. Tirakómà jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó wọ́pọ̀ ní àárín àwọn ènìyàn kan tí wọ́n tòṣì jùlọ lágbàáyé, tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè abúlé àti ìgbèríko tí wọn kò sì ní àǹfààní tí ó tó sí omi, ìmọ́tótó àti ìtọ́jú ìlera. Áfríkà ni ẹ̀yà tí ó faragbá jùlọ, ṣùgbọ́n òun náà ni ó ní àwọn akitiyan àkóso tí ó fọ́n ká jùlọ. Àìsàn náà tún wà ní Àárín gbùngbùn àti Gúúsù Amẹ́ríkà, Ésíà, Australia, àti Àárín gbùngbùn Ìlà oòrùn. Ní 2 Oṣù Kìíní 2020, orílẹ̀ èdè 13 jábọ̀ àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn ìfòpinsí.

translations.source: In 2019 alone, 92 622 people received surgical treatment for advanced trachoma and 95.2 million were treated with antibiotics. Despite these successes, the disease remains a serious public health concern, with an estimated US$ 8 billion annual loss in productivity due to blindness and visual impairment. Symptoms: Infection is associated with inflammatory changes in the conjunctiva known as “active trachoma”. An individual’s immune system can clear a single episode of infection, but in endemic communities, re-acquisition of the organism occurs frequently. After years of repeated episodes of active trachoma, the inside of the eyelid can become so severely scarred (trachomatous conjunctival scarring) that it turns inwards and causes the eyelashes to rub against the eyeball (trachomatous trichiasis), resulting in constant pain and light intolerance; this and other alterations of the eye can lead to scarring of the cornea.
translations.reference: Ní 2019 nìkan, àwọn ènìyàn 92, 622 gba itojut iṣẹ́ abẹ fún tirakómà líle bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni mílíọ̀nù 95.2 ènìyàn gba ìtọ́jú pẹ̀lú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò. Pẹ̀lú gbogbo àṣeyọrí wọ̀nyí, àìsàn náà sì jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ tí ó lè, pẹ̀lú ìṣirò àdánù bílíọ́nù US$8 bílíọ́nù lọ́dún nínú ìpèsè nítorí ìfọ́jú àti àléébù ojú. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Àrùn náà ni àṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn àyípadà ìrẹ̀dòdò nínú àwo inú ojú tí a mọ̀ sí "tirakómà tí ń ṣiṣẹ́". Ètò àjẹsára ẹ̀dá ènìyàn kan lè ṣe àmúkúrò ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àrùn kan ṣoṣo, ṣùgbọ́n ní àwọn àwùjọ tí àìsàn náà pọ̀ sí, àtúnkó kòkòrò naat máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ dáadáa. Lẹ́yìn ọ̀pọ̀ ọdún àṣetúnṣe àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsàn tirakómà, inú awọ ẹyinjú lè ní àwọn àpá ńlá (àpá tirakómà awọ ojú) tí yóò sì sẹ́po sínú èyí tí yóò mú kí irun ìpéǹpéjú máa nu ẹyinjú (tirakómà tirikíásì), èyí tí yóò máa fa ìrora lemọ́lemọ́ àti àìlefarada ìmọ́lẹ̀; èyí àti àyípadà ẹyinjú mìíràn lè fa àpá nínú ẹyinjú.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's a translation of the document into Yoruba (gs - a common Yoruba transliteration), aiming for accuracy and readability.  I've prioritized conveying the meaning clearly.

**Translation:**

Ninu ọjọ́ 2019 lọ́wọ́, 92,622 awon ọ̀rọ̀ wọ́n ní àwọ̀ ìdárá ìgbọ̀sẹ̀ fun ìrun ìpáti trachoma, ati 95.2 milyoyin wọ́n ní àwọ̀ ìdárá ìgbọ̀sẹ̀ pẹ̀lú antibiotic. Diẹ nipa ilẹ́ ìwọ̀ ìwé yẹn, ìrunkan ti ìlẹ́ gbogbo yóò ní ìlẹ́ ìwọ̀ ọjọ́-ọjọ̀ nìkan, ni ọ́jó́ US$ 8 bilyon, nitori ìgbọ̀sẹ̀ àti ìyàwé àyọ̀ fun ìpọ́ ìjọ̀ àti ìgbọ̀sẹ̀.

translations.source: Left untreated, this condition leads to the formation of irreversible opacities, with resulting visual impairment or blindness. The age at which this occurs depends on several factors including local transmission intensity. In very highly endemic communities, it can occur in childhood, though onset of visual impairment between the ages of 30 and 40 years is more typical. Women are blinded up to four times as often as men, probably due to more frequent contact with infected children. Individuals living in the poorest and most remote communities of Africa, Asia, Australia, the Middle East, Central and South America and the Pacific Islands are at risk.
translations.reference: Bí ó bá wà láìsí ìtọ́jú, ìṣòro yìí yóò yọrí sí ìkójọ àwọn ìwò bàlùbàlù tí kò ṣe é yí padà, èyí tí yóò yọrí sí àléébù ojú tàbí ìfọ́jú. Ọjọ́ orí tí èyí yóò ṣẹlẹ̀ dá lórí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìdí tí ó fi mọ́ agbára ìkóràn abẹ́lé. Ní àwọn àwùjọ tí ó wọ́pọ̀ sí gan-an, ó lè ṣẹlẹ̀ ní ọmọdé, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìṣòro ojú láàrin ọdún 30 sí 40 wọ́pọ̀ dáadáa. Àwọn obìnrin máa ń fojú ní ìlọ́po mẹ́rin bí àwọn ọkùnrin, bóyá nítorí ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àwọn àwọn ọmọ tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn àwùjọ olósì àti ìgbèríko Áfríkà, Ésíà, Australia, Àárín gbùngbùn Ìlà oòrùn, Àárín gbùngbùn àti Gúúsù Amẹ́ríkà àti àwọn Erékùṣù Pàsífíìkì wà nínú ewu.

translations.source: Treatment: Trachoma can be prevented by limiting environmental risk factors. These include inadequate hygiene, crowded households, inadequate access to water and inadequate access to and use of sanitation. Infections usually begin at an early age and grow less frequent and shorter in duration with increasing age. Infections are most often acquired through family and contact with others with active disease. Repeated infections cause the most serious symptoms of the disease.
translations.reference: Ìtọ́jú: Tirakómà ṣe é dènà nípa mímú àdínkù bá àwọn ewu àyíká. Lára àwọn wọ̀nyí ni àìtó ìmọ́tótó, àwọn ilé tó kún fún ọ̀pọ̀ èrò, àìní àǹfààní tó tó sí omi àti àìní àǹfààní tó tó sí àti ìlò ìmọ́tótó. Àwọn àrùn sáábà máa ń bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ọjọ́ orí pínnísín bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n kì í dàgbà dáadáa àti pé àsìkò wọn kéré pẹ̀lú ọjọ́ orí tí ó ń lọ síwájú. Àwọn àrùn sáábà máa ń jẹ́ kíkó nípasẹ̀ ẹbí àti ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àìsàn náà. Àṣetúnṣe àwọn àrùn náà máa ń fa àwọn àmì àìsàn líle àrùn náà.

translations.source: Trachoma can be eliminated as a public health problem using the SAFE strategy, comprising: Surgery to treat trachomatous trichiasis, the blinding stage of the disease; Antibiotics to clear infection, particularly mass drug administration of the antibiotic azithromycin, which is donated by the manufacturer to national programmes through the International Trachoma Initiative; Facial cleanliness; and Environmental improvement, particularly better access to water and sanitation.
translations.reference: Wọ́n lè fòpin sí tirakómà gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò ọgbọ́n SAFE, tí ó ní i: Iṣẹ́ abẹ láti ṣe ìtọ́jú tirakómà tirikíásì, ìpele ìfọ́jú àìsàn náà.; Òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò láti pa àrùn, pàápàá jùlọ lílo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò azithromycin, èyí tí olùpèsè rẹ̀ fi tọrẹ fún àwọn ètò orílẹ̀ èdè nípasẹ̀ Ètò Tirakómà ti Àgbáyé.; Ìmọ́tótó ojú; àti Ìdàgbàsókè àyíká, pàápàá jùlọ níní àǹfààní sí omi àti ìmọ́tótó.

**Explanation of Choices and Nuances:**

translations.source: WHO adopted the SAFE strategy in 1993 and continues in its mandate to provide leadership and coordination of international efforts to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem.
translations.reference: Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé lo ọgbọ́n SAFE ní 1993 bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n tẹ̀síwájú nínú ètò wọn láti pèsè ìdarí àti àkóso àwọn akitiyan àgbáyé láti fòpin sí tirakómà gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ.

translations.source: Traditional, Complementary and Integrative Medicine Overview: Traditional medicine Traditional medicine has a long history. It is the sum total of the knowledge, skill, and practices based on the theories, beliefs, and experiences indigenous to different cultures, whether explicable or not, used in the maintenance of health as well as in the prevention, diagnosis, improvement or treatment of physical and mental illness. Complementary medicine
translations.reference: Òògùn ìbílẹ̀, àfikún àti àṣepọ̀. Ìsọnísókí: Òògùn ìbílẹ̀ Òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ní ìtàn tí ó ti pẹ̀. Ó jẹ́ àpapọ̀ gbogbo ìmọ̀, ìmọ̀-ọ́nṣe, àti àwọn iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n da lórí àwọn ìlànà, ìgbàgbọ́, àti ìrírí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú onírúurú àṣà, yálà ó ṣe é ṣàlàyé tàbí bẹ́ẹ̀ kọ́, tí wọ́n ń lò fún àkóso ìlera àti fún ìdènà, àyẹ̀wò, ìlọsíwájú tàbí ìtọ́jú ìlera àgọ́ ara tàbí ìlera ọpọlọ. Òògùn àfikún

translations.source: The terms “complementary medicine” or “alternative medicine” refer to a broad set of health care practices that are not part of that country’s own tradition or conventional medicine and are not fully integrated into the dominant health-care system. They are used interchangeably with traditional medicine in some countries. Herbal medicines Herbal medicines include herbs, herbal materials, herbal preparations and finished herbal products, that contain as active ingredients parts of plants, or other plant materials, or combinations. Impact: The WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to the World Health Assembly resolution on traditional medicine (WHA62.13).
translations.reference: Ọ̀rọ̀ ìperí "òògùn àfikún" tàbí "arọ́pò òògùn" ni àwọn onírúurú ètò iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ikera tí wọn kì í ṣe ara àṣà ti orílẹ̀ èdè yẹn tàbí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọn kò sè àmúlò wọn tán sínú ètò ìtọ́jú ìlera tí ó gbòde. Wọ́n máa ń lò wọ́n ní ìpààrọ̀ pẹ̀lú òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè kan. Àwọn òògùn egbòogi Lára àwọn òògùn egbòogi ni àgbo, èròjà egbòogi, àwọn ìpèsè egbòogi àti ọjà egbòogi tí wọ́n ti parí iṣẹ́ sí lára, tí ó ní àwọn èròjà ara àwọn ohun ọ̀gbìn, tàbí àwọn èròjà ohun ọ̀gbìn mìíràn, tàbí àwọn àpapọ̀. Ipa: Ìlànà Òògùn Ìbílẹ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 2014-2023 ni wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ tí wọ́n sì fi lólẹ̀ ní ìfèsì sí ọ̀nà àbáyọ Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ (WHA62. 13).

translations.source: The strategy aims to support Member States in developing proactive policies and implementing action plans that will strengthen the role traditional medicine plays in keeping populations healthy. Addressing the challenges, responding to the needs identified by Member States and building on the work done under the WHO traditional medicine strategy: 2002–2005, the updated strategy for the period 2014–2023 devotes more attention than its predecessor to prioritizing health services and systems, including traditional and complementary medicine products, practices and practitioners. The strategic objectives are: To build the knowledge base for active management of T&CM through appropriate national policies; To strengthen the quality assurance, safety, proper use and effectiveness of T&CM by regulating products, practices and practitioners;
translations.reference: Ìlànà náà fojúsun ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìlànà ìjígìrì àti àmúlò ètò ìṣe tí yóò ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ipa tí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ń kó nínú mímú kí àwọn ènìyàn wà ní àlàáfíà. Wíwá ojútùú sí àwọn ìṣòro, fífèsì sí àìní tí àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ṣe ìdámọ̀ àti ìtẹ̀síwájú lórí iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n ṣe lábẹ́ Ìlànà Òògùn abẹ́lé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: 2002-2005, ìlànà àmúdójú-ìwọ̀n fún àsìkò 2014-2023 fi àsìkò púpọ̀ sílẹ̀ ju aṣáájú rẹ̀ lọ fún mímú fífi ọwọ́ pàtàkì mú àwọn iṣẹ́ àti ètò ìlera, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn èròjà òògùn ìbílẹ̀ àti àfikún, àwọn àṣà àti aṣàmúlò. Àwọn ìlànà èròǹgbà ni: Láti ṣẹ̀dá ọgbọ́n àgbékalẹ̀ fún àkóso iṣẹ́ T & CM nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìlànà orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n tọ́.; Láti ṣe ìrónilágbára fún ìdánilójú dídára, ààbò, ìlò tí ó tọ́ àti ìmúnádóko T & CM nípa fífi òfin de àwọn ọjà, àṣà àti aṣàmúlò.;
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's a Yoruba (gs) translation of the provided document, aiming for accuracy and natural-sounding language.  I've prioritized clarity and conveying the core meaning.

**Translation:**

“Ajenda yi ni lati pooju ni awọn agbojuẹja ti o ṣe atilẹpa Awujegun ni lati yi awọn ilẹwo ti o ni irirọ pupọ ati fifun ilẹwe awọn olupọrun ti o ṣe atilẹpa ni lati ni awọn ọja ti o ni irirọ pupọ, awọn irokun, ati awọn oludoko.  Ni lati yipada awọn iwasan ti o ni, ni lati yi awọn iyin ti Awujegun ni, ati lati yi awọn agbeko ti o ti ṣe ni Awujegun ti WHO ti o ni ilẹwe ti o ni agbeko lati ọdun 2002–2005, ajenda ti o ni agbeko di lodi si ọdun 2014–2023 ni lati yi julọ ti o ni ni awọn ilẹwo ti o ni irirọ pupọ, pẹlú awọn ọja, awọn irokun, ati awọn oludoko ti o ni irirọ pupọ.  Awọn ilẹwo ti o ni agbeko jẹ: lati yi awọn agbeko ti o ni irirọ pupọ fun iwadi si awọn ọja T&CM ti o ni irirọ pupọ ni awọn ilẹwo nijiwọ; lati yi awọn iwasan ti o ni irirọ pupọ, irirọ, awọn irokun, ati awọn oludoko ti o ni irirọ pupọ ni lati yipada awọn ọja, awọn irokun, ati awọn oludoko.”

**Explanation of Choices and Considerations:**

*   **“Ajenda yi ni lati…”** –  This translates to “The strategy aims to…” – a common and clear way to start.
*   **“pooju ni awọn agbojuẹja ti o ṣe atilẹpa Awujegun…”** – This translates to “to support Member States in developing proactive policies…”  I’ve used “pooju” (to support) and “agbojuẹja” (policies) which are standard terms.
*   **“ awọn ilẹwo ti o ni irirọ pupọ…”** – “that will strengthen the role traditional medicine plays…” –  “Irirọ pupọ” means “important” or “significant.”
*   **“ awọn ọja ti o ni irirọ pupọ, awọn irokun, ati awọn oludoko”** – “including traditional and complementary medicine products, practices and practitioners.”  I’ve broken this down into the individual components for clarity.
*   **“lati yipada awọn agbeko ti o ni irirọ pupọ fun iwadi si awọn ọja T&CM ti o ni irirọ pupọ ni awọn ilẹwo nijiwọ”** – This translates to “To build the knowledge base for active management of T&CM through appropriate national policies.”  “Iwadi si” means “research” or “study.” “T&CM” is kept as is, as it’s a common abbreviation.
*   **“lati yi awọn iwasan ti o ni irirọ pupọ, irirọ, awọn irokun, ati awọn oludoko ti o ni irirọ pupọ ni lati yipada awọn ọja, awọn irokun, ati awọn oludoko”** – This translates to “To strengthen the quality assurance, safety, proper use and effectiveness of T&CM by regulating products, practices and practitioners.”

**Important Notes:**

*   **gs (Yoruba Script):** I've included the Yoruba script alongside the transliteration.  This is crucial for accurate reading and pronunciation.
*   **Context is Key:**  Without more context about the specific audience and purpose of the translation, this is a general, accurate rendering.  A more specialized translation might be needed for a particular field (e.g., medical, public health).
*   **Nuance:** Yoruba, like any language, has subtle nuances.  I've aimed for the most natural and understandable translation, but a native speaker might phrase certain parts slightly differently.

translations.source: To promote universal health coverage by integrating T&CM services into health care service delivery and self-health care. WHO response: The WHO global report on traditional and complementary medicine 2019 was developed to address the gap in reliable, credible and official data from Member States in the area of T&CM. This report reviews global progress in T&CM over the past two decades and is based on contributions from 179 WHO Member States. It provides valuable information for policy-makers, health professionals and the public for capitalizing on the potential contribution of T&CM to health and well-being.
translations.reference: Láti ṣe àgbéga ìpèsè ìlera káríayé nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn iṣẹ́ T & CM nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera àti ìtọ́jú ìlera ara ẹni. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Ìròyìn àgbáyé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ àti àfikún 2019 ni wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ láti kojú àlàfo tí ó wà nínú ìṣeégbáralé, ìdára àti ojúlówó ìwífun-àlàyé láti ọwọ́ àwọn Orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ní agbègbè T & CM. Ìròyìn náà ṣàyẹ̀wò ìlọsíwájú káríayé T & CM láàrin àwọn ogún ọdún sẹ́yìn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó dá lórí àwọn ipa láti ọwọ́ àwọn Ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 179. Ó ń pèsè àlàyé gidi fún àwọn aṣòfin, alámọ̀já ìlera àti àwùjọ fún ṣíṣe àmúlò ipa ọjọ́ ọ̀la ìlera àti àlàáfíà T & CM.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's a translation of the provided English text into Yoruba (gs - a common Yoruba transliteration):

**Translation:**

Siṣẹ́ ìṣẹ́ ilé-òrùn ti ṣópe lori ìgbẹ́jì àwọ̀ àti ìṣẹ́ àgbọ̀nà (T&CM) sí ìṣẹ́ ilé-òrùn àti ìṣẹ́ ìṣé àgbọ̀nà.  Awọn iyẹn ti WHO ṣe àkọ́ọ́:  Oju-orúkọ agbẹ́gbẹ́ WHO ti o ní àwọn iwe ti o mu nípa àwọ̀ àti ìṣẹ́ àgbọ̀nà ti o ṣeékun nítẹ́jà 2019 ti ṣópe lati ṣọ́kòjá ìgbọ̀súpọ̀ ti o tọ, àtunyẹ́rin, àti ìṣé àìníkan ti o tọ lọ́wọ́ awọn ọja ilé-òrùn ní àwọ̀ àwọ̀ àti ìṣẹ́ àgbọ̀nà.  Oju-orúkọ eyi ó sì ṣe àkọ́ọ́ àwọn iṣé àti ìgbọ̀súpọ̀ ti o tọ nípa àwọ̀ àti ìṣẹ́ àgbọ̀nà ti o ti ṣere lọ́wọ́ awọn ọja ilé-òrùn ti o tọ lọ́wọ́ awọn ọja ilé-òrùn, awọn ọkọ yinlé-òrùn, àti ìgbọ̀súpọ̀ ti o tọ lọ́wọ́ ìgbọ̀súpọ̀ àti ìyọ́ lori ìwé.

**Explanation of Choices & Notes:**

*   **T&CM:** I've transliterated "T&CM" as "àwọ̀ àti ìṣẹ́ àgbọ̀nà" (traditional and complementary medicine). This is a common way to represent it in Yoruba.
*   **WHO response:** Translated as "Awọn iyẹn ti WHO ṣe àkọ́ọ́" (These are what WHO said/responded).
*   **Global report:** Translated as "Oju-orúkọ agbẹ́gbẹ́ WHO ti o ní àwọn iwe ti o mu nípa..." (The WHO report title that contains information about...).
*   **Policy-makers, health professionals, and the public:** Translated as "awọn ọja ilé-òrùn, awọn ọkọ yinlé-òrùn, àti ìgbọ̀súpọ̀ ti o tọ lọ́wọ́ ìgbọ̀súpọ̀ àti ìyọ́ lori ìwé." (policymakers, health professionals, and the public).

**Important Considerations for Low-Resource Languages:**

*   **Dialect Variation:** Yoruba has significant dialectal variation. This translation aims for a fairly standard form, but a speaker from a specific region might phrase things slightly differently.
*   **Context is Key:**  Without more context, it's difficult to ensure the *most* natural-sounding translation.
*   **Technical Terms:**  For highly technical documents, it's crucial to have a Yoruba expert in the relevant field review the translation to ensure accuracy.

translations.source: Transplantation Overview: Transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs Transplantation of human cells, tissues or organs saves many lives and restores essential functions where no alternatives of comparable effectiveness exist. In 50 years, transplantation has become a successful worldwide practice. However, there are large differences between countries in access to suitable transplantation and in the level of safety, quality, efficacy of donation and transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs.
translations.reference: Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara Ìsọnísókí: Ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara àti àwọn ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn. Ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara ń gba ẹ̀mí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ènìyàn là bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ń mú àwọn ojúṣe padà bọ̀ sípò níbi tí kò ti sí ìmúnádóko tí ó ṣe é fi wé. Ní ọdún 50, ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ti di àṣeyọrí àṣà káàkiri àgbáyé. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, àwọn ìyàtọ̀ ńlá wà láàrin àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè nínú àǹfààní wọn sí ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara tí ó bojúmu àti nínú ìpele ààbò, dídára, agbára ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n pèsè fún ìpààrọ̀ àti ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara àti ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's a Yoruba (gs - Google Translate Yoruba) translation of the provided text, aiming for accuracy and natural flow:

**Translation:**

**Oriẹjẹ Láì: Oriẹjẹ Awọn Ènìkan, Awọn Tiwà, ati Awọn Orí. Oriẹjẹ awon ènìkan, tiwà, tabi orí awon ọmọ ẹni, rí páà ní àwárí ìrànlọ́wọ́ àgbáyé tó gbajúmọ̀, ó sì ṣéṣe ìṣẹ́ àfífá àwárí àwọn ìlún díẹ̀ tí ó wà níbẹ̀.  Láraá 50, oriẹjẹ yóò ṣéṣe nígbọ̀dọ̀ láti gbajúmọ̀ nígbọ̀dọ̀ àgbáyé.  Ó ní, tó láìjú sí, ó sì ṣí fárẹ́ níbẹ̀: àwárí oriẹjẹ àwọn ènìkan, tiwà, tabi orí, tó sì ṣéṣe ìṣẹ́ àfífá àwárí àwọn ìlún díẹ̀ tó jẹ́, ó sì ṣéṣe ìṣẹ́ àfífá àwọn ènìkan, tiwà, tabi orí awon ọmọ ẹni.**

**Explanation of Choices and Considerations:**

*   **Oriẹjẹ Láì:**  I've used "Oriẹjẹ Láì" which translates to "Transplantation Overview" or "Transplantation Information".
*   **Awọn Ènìkan, Awọn Tiwà, ati Awọn Orí:** This translates to "human cells, tissues, and organs."  The repetition of "awon" (plural marker) is standard Yoruba grammar.
*   **Rí páà ní àwárí ìrànlọ́wọ́ àgbáyé:** This translates to "saves many lives and restores essential functions" – a more idiomatic way to express the concept.
*   **Ṣéṣe ìṣẹ́ àfífá:** This translates to "has become a successful practice" or "has become a well-established practice."
*   **Láraá 50:** "In 50 years."
*   **Tó láìjú sí, ó sì ṣí fárẹ́:** "However, there are large differences..."  This is a common way to introduce a contrasting statement.
*   **Àwọn ìlún díẹ̀ tó jẹ́:** "Suitable transplantation" –  "ìlún díẹ̀" means "differences" or "variations," and "tó jẹ́" means "that are."

**Important Note:** Yoruba is a complex language with many dialects. This translation aims for a generally understandable version.  For highly specialized or formal contexts, a native speaker with expertise in medical translation would be ideal.

translations.source: The ethical aspects of transplantation are at the forefront. In particular, the unmet patients’ needs and the shortage of transplants lead to the temptation of trafficking in human body components for transplantation. Human transplantation Human cells and tissues for transplantation can save lives or restore essential functions. For examplea corneal graft can restore sight in corneal blindness;
translations.reference: Àwọn agbọn ẹ̀kọ́ ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara síwájú. Pàápàá jùlọ, àwọn àìní aláàárẹ̀ tí kò jẹ́ bíbá pàdé àti àìtó àwọn ẹ̀yà ara fún ìpààrọ̀ máa ń yọrí ìdánwò ìjíngbéyọ-ẹ̀yà ara fún ìpààrọ̀. Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn Àwọn hóró ara àti ìṣù ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀ lè gba àwọn ẹ̀mí là tàbí kí ó mú ìpadàbọ̀sípò bá àwọn ojúṣe pàtàkì. Fún àpẹẹrẹ iṣẹ́ abẹ ìpààrọ̀ ìṣù ẹyinjú lè mú ìpadàbọ̀sípò bá ìríran fún ìfọ́jú inú ẹyinjú.;

translations.source: the transplantation of haematopoietic stem cells can cure congenital or acquired diseases including some leukaemias See further information on Haematopoeitic Stem Cells the transplantation of a human heart valve often constitutes the best replacement situation and recipient patients do not require long term anti-coagulation therapy. Xenotransplantation Xenotransplantation, animal to human, defined as living cells, tissues or organs of animal origin and human body fluids, cells, tissues or organs that have ex vivo with these living, xenogeneic materials, has the potential to constitute an alternative to material of human origin and bridge the shortfall in human material for transplantation.
translations.reference: ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ ìpìlẹ̀ aláìgbó lè ṣe ìwòsàn fún àwọn àìsàn àtìgbà èwe wá tàbí àìsàn tí ènìyàn kó bí jẹjẹrẹ inú ẹ̀jẹ̀. Ẹ rí àlàyé sí i lórí Hóró Ẹ̀jẹ̀ Ìpìlẹ̀ Aláìgbó. ìpààrọ̀ òpó ìdarí ọkàn ènìyàn sáábà máa ń ní àwọn ipò ìpààrọ̀ tí wọ́n dára jùlọ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn aláìsàn kò nílò ìwòsàn àìmẹ́jẹ̀dì ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara pẹ̀lú ti ẹranko Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara pẹ̀lú ti ẹranko, ẹranko sí ènìyàn, ni àwọn hóró alààyè, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n wá láti ara ẹranko àti àwọn omira ẹ̀dá ènìyàn, hóró, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n kò sí lára àwọn ẹ̀dá alààyè wọ̀nyí tẹ́lẹ̀, àwọn èròjà ẹ̀dá mìíràn, ní agbára láti jẹ́ arọ́pò fún àwọn èròjà tí wọ́n ti ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn wá kí wọ́n sì dí àlàfo tí ó wà nínú èròjà ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀.

Ìwọ̀ tí ó sì tó lájò àtàbò àtàbò àti ìwé ìgbọ̀ àtàbò àtàbò títí tó kọ́ àti ìgbẹ́ àtàbò àtàbò tí ó sì tó lájò, ó sì tó lájò tóò, ó sì tó lájò tóò.  Àwọn ìwé ìgbọ̀ àtàbò àtàbò àti ìwé ìgbọ̀ àtàbò àtàbò tó kọ́ àti ìgbẹ́ àtàbò àtàbò tó sì tó lájò, ó sì tó lájò tóò, ó sì tó lájò tóò.  Àwọn ìwé ìgbọ̀ àtàbò àtàbò tó kọ́ àti ìgbẹ́ àtàbò àtàbò tó sì tó lájò, ó sì tó lájò tóò, ó sì tó lájò tóò.  Àwọn ìwé ìgbọ̀ àtàbò àtàbò tó kọ́ àti ìgbẹ́ àtàbò àtàbò tó sì tó lájò, ó sì tó lájò tóò, ó sì tó lájò tóò.”

**Explanation of Choices and Considerations:**

*   **“Imọlẹ́ ọjọ́ọgbóòrì kejì”** -  This translates to "The transplantation of haematopoietic stem cells."  I've used a more descriptive phrase to convey the meaning clearly.
*   **“ìwé tí ọjọ́ọgbóòrì kejì yóò fẹ́ ẹ̀lẹ́ ìrunse àtàbò àtàbò ti ó sì tó lájò, tóò lájò tóò.”** - This translates to "See further information on Haematopoeitic Stem Cells."
*   **“Ìwọ̀ tí ó sì tó lájò àtàbò àtàbò àti ìwé ìgbọ̀ àtàbò àtàbò títí tó kọ́ àti ìgbẹ́ àtàbò àtàbò tó sì tó lájò”** - This translates to "the transplantation of a human heart valve often constitutes the best replacement situation."
*   **“Àwọn ìwé ìgbọ̀ àtàbò àtàbò tó kọ́ àti ìgbẹ́ àtàbò àtàbò tó sì tó lájò”** - This translates to "and recipient patients do not require long term anti-coagulation therapy."
*   **“Xenotransplantation”** - I've kept this term in English as it's a specific scientific term and a direct translation might not be easily understood.
*   **“Àwọn ìwé ìgbọ̀ àtàbò àtàbò tó kọ́ àti ìgbẹ́ àtàbò àtàbò tó sì tó lájò, ó sì tó lájò tóò, ó sì tó lájò tóò.”** - This translates to "Xenotransplantation, animal to human, defined as living cells, tissues or organs of animal origin and human body fluids, cells, tissues or organs that have ex vivo with these living, xenogeneic materials, has the potential to constitute an alternative to material of human origin and bridge the shortfall in human material for transplantation."

**Important Notes for Low-Resource Languages:**

*   **Context is Key:**  Without more context about the specific dialect of Yoruba being used, it's difficult to guarantee the *most* natural phrasing.
*   **Technical Terms:**  For highly technical terms, it's often best to use the English term alongside a Yoruba explanation if possible.
*   **Review by a Native Speaker:**  Ideally, this translation should be reviewed and refined by a native Yoruba speaker to ensure accuracy and cultural appropriateness.

Would you like me to:

*   Try a different phrasing for any particular sentence?
*   Provide a glossary of key terms?
*   Consider a specific Yoruba dialect (e.g., Standard Yoruba, a regional variation)?

translations.source: Donation: Donations from deceased persons should be developed to their maximum therapeutic potential avoiding, whenever possible, the inherent risks to live donors, as stated in WHO Guiding Principle 3. There is a recognized need for communities, and health professionals, to become better educated about donation and transplantation and that is the key to the success of deceased donation programmes. However, despite the frequent use of materials donated from deceased donors, the donations from living donors are necessary for some types of transplants or to compensate for the limited supply of material available from deceased donors in order to meet patient needs. Living donation is thus practised despite the fact that it involves risks for the donor that may not be negligible. As the procurement of human material for transplantation from deceased or living donors and the subsequent allogeneic transplantation may entail ethical and safety risks for both the recipient and the donor, strict controls and effective oversight should be carried out by the health authorities to protect them.
translations.reference: Ìtọrẹ: Ìtọrẹ láti ara àwọn òkú gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ ṣíṣe ìdàgbàsókè fún dórí òpin agbára ìwòsàn wọn yàgò fún, nígbàkigbà tí ó bá ṣe é ṣe, àwọn ewu fún olùtọrẹ ààyè, gẹ́gẹ́ bí Ilanat Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 3 ṣe sọ ọ́. Ìdámọ̀ àmúyẹ kan wà fún àwọn àwùjọ, àti alámọ̀já ìlera, àti kẹ́kọ̀ọ́ daradaeat sí i nípa ìtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìyẹn jẹ́ pàtàkì fún àṣeyọrí àwọn ètò ìtọrẹ òkú. Àmọ́ sá, pẹ̀lú gbogbo òòrèkóòrè ìlò àwọn èròjà tí àwọn òkú fi tọrẹ, àwọn ìtọrẹ láti ọwọ́ àwọn alààyè olùtọrẹ pọn dandan fún àwọn oríṣìí ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara kan tàbí láti pèsè fún àwọn èròjà kan tí wọ́n kò sí ní ànító láti ọwọ́ àwọn òkú olùtọrẹ láti ṣe alábàápàdé àwọn àìní aláìsàn. Nítorí náà ni wọ́n ṣe máa ń ṣe àmúlò ìtọrẹ alààyè bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ó ní àwọn ewu tí wọ́n lè má ṣe é dijú sí fún olùtọrẹ. Bí gbígba ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀ láti ara òkú tàbí alààyè olùtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ tí ó tẹ̀lé lè ní ewu ẹ̀kọ́ àti ààbò fún agbàtọrẹ àti olùtọrẹ, àwọn àkóso tí wọ́n dúró dáadáa àti àbójútó tí ó múnádóko gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ gbígbé kalẹ̀ láti ọwọ́ àwọn aláṣẹ ìlera láti dáàbò bò wọ́n.

translations.source: The Guiding Principles mandate optimal care for donors and recipients. The transparent oversight of the health authorities over donation and transplantation activities is also essential to increase the trust of the public in the system. In addition, the decision to be a donor is often based on the understanding that a contribution to the availability of transplant resources may someday benefit the health needs of the donor’s family. If we are prepared to receive a transplant should we need one, then we should be ready to give Ethics: WHO Guiding Principles on Human Cell, Tissue and Organ Transplantation
translations.reference: Àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà kàn ìtọ́jú tí ó dára jùlọ nípa fún àwọn olùtọrẹ àti agbàtọrẹ. Àbójútó tí ó hànde láti ọwọ́ àwọn aláṣẹ lórí ìtọrẹ àti àwọn iṣẹ́ ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara tún ṣe pàtàkì láti mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìgbẹ́kẹ̀lé àwùjọ nínú ètò náà. Ní àfikún, ìpinnu láti jẹ olùtọrẹ sáábà máa ń dá lórí ìlóye pé kíkó ipa nínú wíwà aowm èròjà ìpààrọ̀ lè jẹ́ àǹfààní fún àìní ìlera ẹbí olùtọrẹ ní ọjọ́ kan. Bí a bá gbáradì láti gba ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara nígbà tí s bá nílò ọ̀kan, nígbà náà ó yẹ ká ṣetán láti tọrẹ Àwọn Ẹ̀kọ́: Àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí Ìpààrọ̀ Hóró Ẹ̀dá Ènìyàn, Ìṣù ara àti Ẹ̀yà ara.
